sentences.text: Before I narrate about our second arrival to Stanislau, I want to mention I forgot to give the date when my father died in 1943.
It was March 25th on the [?
Julian ?] calendar, and it was 18 days [HEBREW] on the Hebrew calendar.
We are now in 1943.
We were shipped out from Hungary as a labor unit to the Eastern front.
Our first stop is the city of Stanislau.
I have to mention that [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] we all knew that shipping out to the Eastern front was a very, very dangerous journey.

When we were in [INAUDIBLE] before-- before we were shipped out, there came a representative of a church-- I don't remember of what church-- and they proposed to whoever would convert to Christianity would be taken out of this camp, labor camp, and put in a special camp with no yellow bands.
I have to say-- out of the 200 and [? twenty-some ?] boys that were in this group, only one boy acquiesced to that proposition, and he was taken out.
Because we knew that once we were shipped out to the Eastern front, our whole existence depended entirely on the commander of our-- of our company.
Now, in spite of that great fear, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we relied on our destiny.
Whatever will be will be.
And we arrived to the city of Stanislau.
We were put in empty houses that were Jewish houses before.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was the first couple of days that we were there.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We all knew that the city of Stanislau had a very large Jewish community.
And here we saw, when he came in there, we saw this terrible destruction.
But for us, for me personally, we were there before, and we knew some of it, but we didn't know in this one and a half years since we were there before that it was such a total-- total destruction.
After a few days when we arrived in Stanislau, the commander started sending out certain groups with Hungarian escorts to do certain works.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] One group was sent out that worked in-- in-- where they cleaned-- they cleaned carcasses for leather from cattle.
That was very, very dirty work.
Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to-- I want to-- I want to fulfill the promise that I promised this girl and what I saw in there the first week when we arrived there.
And I have to read.
This is just one page. 'One day, I fell into a group of five soldiers of our [INAUDIBLE] escorted us to a four-story building in the city of Stanislau.
The building was an officer's club for Hungarian and German officers.
The building had no elevator, only stairs, and our job was to wash and clean the whole stairway, and also some rooms-- to wash the floors.
As we reached the top floor, we saw in the rooms lots of young women.
We didn't catch on right away what was going on there.
We, the Carpathian boys, we spoke among ourselves Yiddish.
When one of the girls overheard us, that we talked in Yiddish, she broke out in a lament in tears, and she started to talk to us and tell us that they are simply here for pure rape for these animals.
And after sometimes, these girls disappear and they never come back here.
They're not even allowed to go down to the third floor.
God in heaven, this face from this Jewish girl, I will never forget.
The deep anguish and her pain, I saw in her eyes.
Some of the girls, she told me, were throwing themselves down the windows.
They could not-- down the windows [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the fourth floor.
After that happened, they secured the windows that they could not open it anymore.
And they also put up a 24-hour guard on the floor.
This poor girl cried with bloody tears, and she asked me and said, if you survive the war, tell our story.
Tell the world what these beasts did to us.
In truth, it is unfair to the animals to call the German rapists animals.
Male animals don't rape female of their own species.
My dear Jewish sister, I'm fulfilling now your request.
I looked into your eyes and I saw and felt your pain, and I still am feeling the pain.
And I will as long as I'll live.'
This is the memoirs that I have written from my experience in the war.
And I wrote this here.
This is what--
I wrote this about six years ago.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After-- after we spent in Stanislau, we spent about four to six weeks.
We were sent out to all kinds of different works.
We mostly were sent to the station where we did some loading, where the train station were loading material that was going in the direction and in the opposite direction.
There were goods going back and forth.
[SIGHS] From Stanislau, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were loaded up [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and we were sent [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] to a city closer to the battle line.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- we-- we-- we crossed-- we crossed the the Bug River to the city that was called Brest-Litovsk.
The Poles called it [POLISH].
This city is very famous-- was famous for a peace treaty that the Germans dictated to the Russians in the first World War.
And there is a citadel there.
It's called the Brest Citadel.
And the peace treaty-- that treaty, I think, it was in 1917-- it was called the Brest [POLISH] Treaty.

The reason why they sent us over there was that the train that supplied the lines, the German armies, passed through a forest that was 20 or 30 kilometers long through the forest, and the soil in that area is very swampy and sandy-- very, very soft.

And the partisans, Russian partisans, that were operating in these forests had a ball of being hidden in the forest and to lay mines under those lines and to blast the German trains that were shipping in-- were going in-- with material.
It was very easy because the soil was soft and you could one, two, three, dig out under the ties to put a mine under there and blow up the trains.

The Germans were suffering heavily from the partisans blowing up these trains, and they decided that the only way to get rid of that is to cut completely 1/2 a kilometer on each side of the-- of the rails.
Cut it down so that nobody that can hide so close that they would see.

Besides what they did was that each locomotive had a few-- a few empty cars-- they were not empty in the front of the locomotive-- that were loaded with stone, just heavy, in order when they would go through and this would blow up, it would only hurt the empty-- the empty [INAUDIBLE] and it would not destroy the material.
But the idea was that they will put in some 10,000 of those Hungarian/Jewish labor units, and they would cut the entire forest down to the ground.
That would solve their problem with the partisans.
And when it was warm, you know, we didn't get shoes or clothing.
We had what we had and it had to last.
So this was a terrible place to work.

If it was cold and it was frozen, it wasn't so bad, but when it got a little bit warmer, you know, when it got in January, February, it got a little bit warmer, you were in-- in the-- in the mud all the time.
You had to drag these lumbers and load it on trains.
This was good lumber there, pine.
But we weren't able to-- we were-- they put us in in barns, and we weren't able to dry out our clothing.
And many of the-- many of the boys were just getting flu, typhus, and they were falling like flies.
Fortunately, this lasted two or three months, and I managed to get through those two, three months more or less.
I had a [INAUDIBLE], a pair of good shoes, and we were able to talk to the local people.
By the way, the local population there, the White Russians, they were-- they were-- they kept the [? subbotnik ?] religion.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's a Christian religion, but they kept the Sabbath.
I don't understand it too well, but anyway, the people were friendly to us over there.

And I remember where we stayed in one of the barns, when I talked to the owner, I asked him if he is able to sell me some linen, pieces of linen, so that we could at least have dry linen because socks we didn't have.
We would just tie around the linen in the shoe.
At least you had dry linen.
And they did that for us in that winter.
The city changed hands about two, three times.
The Germans would be pushed back, then the Germans would push the Russians back.
It was a very, very bad time.
From after we got finished-- after we got finished cutting down this forest, we tried to somehow get in touch with the partisans because we were considering of deserting in there.
And it turned out that we did get contact with them through the local people, but they would-- they would not accept anybody unless we could bring with us weapons.
Without weapons, they would not accept.
And we had no way of getting not even a revolver or anything because we didn't have it.
This was on the other side of the Bug River.

It was assumed that if-- if the Germans-- if the Russians would push the Germans back behind the Bug River, they would put up-- they would put up a stand at the river because it's a fairly big river and that would be a good line-- a line of defense.
He was the one that happened to be out on the outhouse when this happened, and he survived the war.
He-- I talked-- I talked to him because I wanted the name-- it was very-- in all the units, we heard about it, and we were really scared.
Because as I said, we were totally depending on the-- on the commander.
The commander had-- life and death was in his hands.
Whatever he wanted to do, nobody-- there was no law-- no law to protect-- protect us.
And we came to this place, and we-- we-- we-- they stopped us, and we just saw German high officers put out tables in the middle of the street and looking at maps, and we thought we were surrounded.
We were hoping that if the Russians would liberate us.
We didn't know what the Russians did.

We found out after the war, then, they liberated-- I have a cousin that fell in-- that in those camps, the Russians, they treated him-- they treated these people like they treated the other-- the other poor prisoners.
